data.title: Trump praises DR Congo and Rwanda as they sign US-mediated peace deal
data.description:
US President Donald Trump praised the leaders of DR Congo and Rwanda for their courage as they signed a deal on Thursday aimed at ending the conflict in eastern Congo and opening the region’s critical mineral reserves to the US government and American companies.
The moment offered Trump – who has repeatedly and with a measure of exaggeration boasted of brokering peace in some of the world’s most entrenched conflicts – another chance to tout himself as a deal maker extraordinaire on the global stage and make the case that he is deserving of the Nobel Peace Prize. The US leader has not been shy about his desire to receive the honour.
“It’s a great day for Africa, a great day for the world,” Trump said before the leaders signed the pact. “Today, we’re succeeding where so many others have failed.”
Trump welcomed Presidents Felix Tshisekedi of the Democratic Republic of Congo and Paul Kagame of Rwanda, as well as several officials from other African nations who travelled to Washington to witness the signing, in the same week he contemptuously derided the war-torn country of Somalia and said he did not want immigrants from the East African nation in the US.
Lauded by the White House as a “historic” agreement brokered by Trump, the pact between Tshisekedi and Kagame follows months-long peace efforts by the US and partners, including the African Union and Qatar, and finalises an earlier deal signed in June.
But the Trump-brokered peace is precarious.
The Central African nation of Congo has been battered by decades-long fighting with more than 100 armed groups, the most potent being the Rwanda-backed M23 rebels. The conflict escalated this year, with M23 seizing the region’s main cities of Goma and Bukavu in an unprecedented advance, worsening a humanitarian crisis that was already one of the world’s largest, with millions of people displaced.

Fighting, meanwhile, continued this week in the conflict-battered region with pockets of clashes reported between the rebels and Congolese soldiers, together with their allied forces. Trump, a Republican, has often said that his mediation has ended the conflict, which some people in Congo say is not true.
Still, Kagame and Tshisekedi offered a hopeful tone as they signed on to the agreement.
“No one was asking President Trump to take up this task. Our region is far from the headlines,” Kagame said. “But when the president saw the opportunity to contribute to peace, he immediately took it.”
“I do believe this day is the beginning of a new path, a demanding path, yes. Indeed, quite difficult,” Tshisekedi said. “But this is a path where peace will not just be a wish, an aspiration, but a turning point.”
Indeed, analysts say Thursday’s deal also is not expected to quickly result in peace. A separate peace deal has been signed between Congo and the M23.
But Trump predicted with the signing the countries would leave behind “decades of violence and bloodshed” and “begin a new year of harmony and cooperation”.
“They spent a lot of time killing each other,” Trump said. “And now they’re going to spend a lot of time hugging, holding hands and taking advantage of the United States of America economically like every other country does.”
Tshisekedi and Kagame did not shake hands and barely looked at each other during the roughly 50-minute signing ceremony.

Thursday’s pact will also build on a Regional Economic Integration Framework previously agreed upon that officials have said will define the terms of economic partnerships involving the three countries.
Trump also announced the US was signing bilateral agreements with the nations that will unlock new opportunities for the US to access critical minerals – deals that will benefit all three economies.
“Everybody’s going to make a lot of money,” Trump said.
The region, rich in critical minerals, has been of interest to Trump as Washington looks for ways to circumvent China to acquire rare earths, essential to manufacturing fighter jets, mobile phones and more. China accounts for nearly 70 per cent of the world’s rare earth mining and controls roughly 90 per cent of global rare earths processing.
Trump hosted the leaders on Thursday morning for one-on-one meetings at the White House as well as a three-way conversation before the signing ceremony at the Institute of Peace in Washington, which the State Department announced on Wednesday has been rebranded “the Donald J. Trump Institute of Peace”.
In eastern Congo, meanwhile, residents reported pockets of clashes and rebel advances in various localities. Both the M23 and Congolese forces have accused each other of violating the terms of the ceasefire agreed earlier this year. Fighting has also continued in the central plateaus across South Kivu province.
The conflict can be traced to the aftermath of the 1994 genocide in Rwanda, where Hutu militias killed between 500,000 and 1 million ethnic Tutsi, as well as moderate Hutus and Twa, indigenous people. When Tutsi-led forces fought back, nearly 2 million Hutus crossed into Congo, fearing reprisals.
Rwandan authorities have accused the Hutus who fled of taking part in the genocide and alleged that elements of the Congolese army protected them. They say the militias formed by a small fraction of the Hutus are a threat to Rwanda’s Tutsi population.
Congo’s government says there cannot be permanent peace if Rwanda does not withdraw its support troops and other support for the M23 in the region. Rwanda, on the other hand, has conditioned a permanent ceasefire on Congo dissolving a local militia that it said is made up of the Hutus and is fighting with the Congolese military.
The UN has said that between 3,000 and 4,000 Rwandan government forces are deployed in eastern Congo, operating alongside the M23. Rwanda denies such support but says any action taken in the conflict is to protect its territory.

data.title: Chinese shipyards shift focus to advanced vessels as South Korean rivals gain ground
data.description:
Chinese shipyards, facing a drop in orders, plan to divert more capacity towards new-energy and advanced vessels to maintain profitability as they face mounting challenges from South Korean counterparts, according to industry officials.
The country’s shipbuilding firms, from gargantuan state-owned China State Ship­building Corp (CSSC) to privately owned shipyards, held 65 per cent of global orders in deadweight tonnage terms during the first three quarters of 2025, compared with about 75 per cent a year earlier, according to shipping services provider Clarksons.
Geopolitical tensions resulted in a plunge in orders for Chinese-made vessels this year, which stood at 10.5 million tonnes in the three months to September, down 61 per cent from 26.9 million tonnes a year earlier, according to Clarksons.
“The world economy and shipbuilding industry have entered a new cycle where growth will slow and uncertainties will increase,” said Xing Wenhua, chairman of the Shanghai Society of Naval Architects and Marine Engineers. “China’s shipyards have displayed their resilience and are playing a role in stabilising the global [shipbuilding] supply chain.”

Earlier this year, the US threatened to slap port fees on Chinese vessels amid an escalating trade war between the world’s two largest economies. In October, the Trump administration slapped a levy of US$50 per net tonne on Chinese vessels before Beijing retaliated with a 400 yuan (US$57) per net tonne charge for US ships.
Beijing and Washington agreed to drop the punitive fees after they reached a truce in the trade war at the end of October.
South Korea’s shipbuilding orders jumped this year, with its share of the global market nearly doubling to 25.9 per cent in the first three quarters from 13.3 per cent a year ago.
Gao Dongsheng, chief economist at the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, told the ongoing Marintec China conference in Shanghai on Tuesday that China hoped to build several world-class shipbuilders while creating a more integrated supply chain, amid the increasing use of artificial intelligence and new-energy technologies in next-generation vessels.
In the first half of 2025, the country’s shipbuilders reported a combined revenue of 398.8 billion yuan, up 20.8 per cent from a year earlier. Gross profit margin reached an all-time high of 9.7 per cent, according to Tan Naifen, deputy secretary general of the China Association of the National Shipbuilding Industry (CANSI).
“The Chinese government is fully aware of the risks in blindly expanding manufacturing capacity in various industries, and officials are urging shipbuilders to focus on quality and profitability, rather than quantity,” said Gao Shen, an independent analyst in Shanghai. “After all, China remains the world’s largest shipbuilding nation with a lion’s share of the market.”
China’s shipbuilding industry suffered significant setbacks after the global financial crisis in 2008, which led to a downturn in the maritime sector worldwide. About half of the country’s shipbuilding capacity, which reached 80 million deadweight tonnes at its peak, became redundant, while many shipyards struggled to turn a profit, according to CANSI.
On Monday, Ma Yunxiang, assistant president of CSSC, the world’s largest shipbuilding conglomerate controlling one-third of the global market, said the group was looking to build more cruise ships, deep-ocean drilling ships and nuclear-powered vessels as part of efforts to diversify its portfolio and move up the value chain.
The company is now constructing Adora Flora City, mainland China’s second self-built cruise ship, which is due to be delivered next year.
It also delivered deep-ocean drilling ship Mengxiang, the mainland’s first vessel of its kind, in late 2024. The vessel is capable of navigating against extreme wind conditions and could drill as deep as 11,000 metres.
CSSC is exhibiting its latest technologies at Marintec, the world’s largest maritime conference and exhibition, which covers a total floor space of 110,000 square metres at the Shanghai New International Expo Centre and has attracted an unprecedented 2,200 exhibitors. The expo ends on Friday.

data.title: Hong Kong music icon Alan Tam: from Leslie Cheung’s rival to fans smashing his albums
data.description:
This is the 65th instalment in a biweekly series profiling major Hong Kong pop culture figures of recent decades.
For over half a century, Alan Tam Wing-lun has stood as a colossus in Hong Kong show business.
To his legions of fans, he is Principal Tam – a moniker that denotes a kind of institutional authority over the city’s music industry. As lead vocalist of The Wynners, he defined the sound of a generation; as a solo singer, he set records that remain impressive; and as a mentor, he has shepherded countless young stars into the limelight.
With more than 115 solo albums and sales exceeding 35 million records worldwide, the Cantopop heavyweight has cemented his position as an icon in Hong Kong’s entertainment history.

However, in recent years, the legacy of the 75-year-old Tam – famous for his quip that his youthful look made him “forever 25” – has been tested by intense scrutiny over sensational personal rumours and polarising political statements.
Born in Hong Kong in 1950 as the only son among six siblings, Tam inherited discipline and athleticism from his father, Tam Kong-pak, a celebrated football player who represented China in the 1936 Berlin Olympics.
While the 1970s are now revered as the golden age of Cantopop, it was also a decade of identity formation for Hong Kong, and Tam was at the forefront of this cultural shift.
Before The Wynners, Tam honed his craft as a lead singer in the band’s original iteration, The Loosers, which was formed in 1969 by several future industry veterans: Tam, Anthony Chan Yau, Bennett Pang Kin-san, Danny Yip Chi-keung, Nat Chan Pak-cheung and his brother, Sam Chan Pak-sun.

Reflecting the colonial atmosphere, the group drew heavily from Western pop culture, covering The Beatles and The Rolling Stones in their bar gigs. “Back then, we sang English songs first, and then gradually Cantonese songs,” Tam said in a 2014 interview with Shanghai publication Jiemian.
Despite their growing momentum, the group disbanded in 1972 when Tam left to study in Singapore. Upon his return the following year, he reunited with Pang, Anthony Chan and Yip to reform the band, this time with the addition of Kenny Bee. They also changed the band name at the suggestion of DJ Uncle Ray (Ray Cordeiro).
The “Five Tigers” of The Wynners soon became the defining music idols of 1970s Hong Kong. With their fashionably long hair, bell-bottom jeans and platform shoes, they struck a chord with a youth culture hungry for local heroes.
“Music was simpler back then – there weren’t as many genres and the audience’s demands weren’t high. It was all about entertainment,” Tam said in 2014.

Despite reaching new heights with every project, the members went their separate ways in 1978, instead promising to reunite every five years – a promise Tam reflected on during a 2023 concert, saying, “It’s always nostalgic whenever the band gets together because we are like a family.”
Without the constraints of a group, Tam dominated the late 1970s and 1980s. He released his first solo album, Naughty Boy, in 1979; consisting mainly of adapted English pop songs, it achieved gold record sales. His follow-up in 1980, Love You to the Point of Madness, scored platinum sales.
Tam’s signature style crystallised during this period: romantic ballads featuring modern, often Western-influenced arrangements and polished production. Radio favourites like “Love in Autumn” (1984), “Love in the Fog” (1984) and “Love Trap” (1986) propelled him to superstardom.
Tam secured the “Most Popular Male Artist” title at TVB’s Jade Solid Gold Awards for four consecutive years from 1984 to 1987. The scale of his fan base was most evident in the summer of 1985, when he held 20 concerts with 260,000 attendees, setting a new benchmark for solo concerts by a Hong Kong singer.

Tam’s chart domination sparked one of the most intense chapters in Hong Kong entertainment history: the rivalry between him and Leslie Cheung Kwok-wing. Although they maintained a respectful relationship, their perceived feud stirred intense competition for music awards, sales figures and fan adoration.
Just when the pressure of this fan war reached its boiling point in 1988, however, Tam shocked the industry by announcing that he would no longer accept music awards.
“This does not mean I am retiring, because I can’t bear to lose [my fans],” he added on stage at the 10th Top Ten Chinese Gold Songs Awards.
Tam’s decision was made ostensibly to give opportunities to newcomers, but was widely interpreted as a move to de-escalate the toxic rivalry.

The two formally buried the hatchet in 1999, when Cheung contributed to an album paying tribute to Tam by taking part in a medley remix of Tam’s popular songs, which he performed alongside 17 other singers.
Tam also looked beyond the confines of the recording studio. In 1979, he started pursuing opportunities in Taiwan, starring in over 10 films, including several by renowned director Pai Ching-jui.
His breakthrough came with 1981’s If I Were for Real, a Taiwanese drama laden with anti-communist commentary that was banned by mainland Chinese censors. He won the best actor prize at the 18th Golden Horse Awards in Taiwan for the part.
As Tam transitioned from Cantopop’s reigning monarch to a respected mentor role over the years, he earned the nickname “Principal Tam”. Elected president of the Hong Kong Performing Artistes Guild in 2008, he also became a fixture on TVB singing contests.

However, he faced fierce backlash from some quarters during the anti-government protests in June 2019, when he – along with Kenny Bee – attended a rally in support of the Hong Kong police and condemned the protesters.
Some fans publicly smashed his albums, while others petitioned to strip him of his Golden Horse award for If I Were for Real, highlighting the irony of a pro-establishment figure holding an award for an anti-communist satire.
Tam’s personal life has faced a similar spotlight. He is known to have started an extramarital affair in 1991 and had a son born out of wedlock in 1996, despite remaining married to Sally Yeung Kit-mei, his lawful wife since 1981.
“It’s not good to have two wives,” Tam said in a radio interview in 2020, when he reflected on his long-term relationship with his mistress.

In 2021, rumours swirled around an alleged rendezvous with a young female fan from mainland China after a Weibo user posted photos of a partly undressed man that he alleged to be Tam.
While the allegation was denied by the singer, it nonetheless popularised the humorous slang phrase “eating vermicelli” – meaning sleeping with fans (as “vermicelli” and “fans” are homophones in Cantonese) – in the local pop culture lexicon.
Yet, despite the bad press, Tam seems to have reached such rarefied heights as a cultural icon that he stays largely impervious to scandal. He kicked off his Alan Tam Classic Live Tour in 2023, performing across Asia and Australia, with the tour continuing into 2025.
Asked in 2014 what his best life decision was, Tam said it was simply to make music.
“We [The Wynners] initially thought it was a very short-term job, that we would sing until we were 30, but we still haven’t retired yet,” he laughed.
He is still going strong more than five decades later.
Want more articles like this? Follow SCMP Film on Facebook

data.title: China’s low-cost AI is showing up America’s high-cost tech model
data.description:
Whenever an American breakthrough in artificial intelligence (AI) is announced, China is not far behind. That has long alarmed policymakers and industry insiders in the United States. Whether by handicapping China with dodgy trade sanctions and export restrictions with its allies, or handing out billions of taxpayer dollars to favoured industry players, the US just can’t shake off its Chinese competitors.
China’s DeepSeek seemingly came out of nowhere when it launched in late 2023 a free, open-source AI model that rivalled OpenAI’s popular ChatGPT model, developed at a fraction of the US company’s cost. Now, it has launched its most powerful model variant, DeepSeek-V3.2-Speciale, said to match Google DeepMind’s new Gemini 3 Pro, a leading AI model, in certain key tasks.
Of course, other Chinese companies such as Alibaba Group Holding, which owns the Post, are also making big strides. After its public beta launch last month, the Qwen app, which uses Alibaba’s most advanced open-source AI model, quickly became one of the top three most downloaded free apps in China on Apple app stores. A research team from Alibaba Cloud was also one of four to be awarded best paper at NeurIPS, the annual Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems in the US for groundbreaking research on large language models (LLMs), the foundation of AI.
At a comparatively low cost and with less computing power, DeepSeek’s V3.2-Speciale matches the Gemini 3 Pro in reasoning capabilities and offers efficient results. It has scaled up less powerful chips and made them work together to rival the most advanced and expensive ones that power American models. Chinese AI developers have been able to do more with less, which amounts to a fundamental challenge to the development and investment models of AI in the US.
The American notion is that AI is capital-intensive, perhaps more so than any other US industry today. This has meant the need for trillion-dollar investments to develop the most advanced chips and build data centres that eat up water and power supplies. Some of those investments have led to accusations of circular or self-reinforcing deals. The fear is that the AI investment craze has become a bubble waiting to burst. Indeed, the US stock market has been held up by a handful of AI-led tech firms with trillion-dollar valuations. Whether they are really worth that much is anyone’s guess.
The real China threat here is that it has exposed America’s AI myths by showing what can be achieved at much lower costs and with greater efficiency, while offering the technology as open source. Perhaps China has the US chip war to thank for developing its own world-class AI industry.

data.title: Honduran centrist Nasralla alleges vote fraud as Trump-backed Asfura edges ahead
data.description:
Honduran centrist candidate Salvador Nasralla alleged fraud in the country’s highly contested presidential vote on Thursday after his Trump-backed rival Nasry Asfura pulled narrowly past him overnight.
Nasralla, in a post on social media, said the screen displaying the vote data went blank at 3.24am and alleged “an algorithm changed the data”, giving the higher tally to Asfura after Nasralla had been leading the vote count since Tuesday. The results are being updated on the electoral body’s website.
The run-up to the vote in the small Central American country had been rocked for months by allegations of fraud. The election was catapulted to the international stage as US President Donald Trump threw his support behind Asfura and alleged, without evidence, that there had been possible fraud in the initial vote tally.
The National Party’s Asfura held 40.08 per cent, about 10,100 votes ahead of the Liberal Party’s Nasralla, who had 39.70 per cent. Rixi Moncada of the ruling leftist LIBRE Party remained well behind in third place in the election, which was held on Sunday.
With about 85 per cent of the tally sheets counted, each representing votes from a single polling station, Asfura gained a narrow lead. Around 17 per cent of the tally sheets have inconsistencies and will be reviewed, according to the country’s electoral authority.
Nasralla’s post did not include evidence of the vote change.
“They must investigate the Colombian company involved in these changes, ASD,” he said. Fraud allegations have haunted Honduras since the fiercely contested 2013 presidential election, when opposition leaders accused the ruling party of manipulating vote tallies and violating campaign finance rules in a race marred by irregularities.
Nasralla said Honduras “will not allow a repeat of the Batson curve”, a reference to David Matamoros Batson, the former electoral tribunal chief whose tenure became synonymous with late-night vote swings and contested results in past elections.
ASD, the Colombian company handling Honduras’ ballot processing, did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Ana Paola Hall of the electoral body said earlier on Thursday the margin between the National and Liberal Party candidates was historically thin and commended the candidates for refraining from “declarations of victory or any attempts at sabotage” in a post on social media.

The latest tally marks another shift in the see-saw race. Nasralla had held a razor-thin lead on Wednesday and the two front runners have repeatedly switched places as hand-counted tallies trickled in from across the country.
Preliminary results released on Monday showed what the electoral body called a “technical tie”.
Voting on Sunday was calm and peaceful, according to independent electoral observers. But the subsequent reporting of the results has been chaotic, marred by starts and stops that have intensified frustrations over the tight race.
Members of the electoral council have blamed the company behind the tabulating platform for pauses in the vote count.
Trump has repeatedly waded into Honduras’ election. Ahead of the vote, he strongly backed Asfura, the 67-year-old former mayor of the capital of Tegucigalpa.
Then he claimed, without providing evidence, possible fraud in a social media post on Monday night.
Trump also pardoned a former president of Asfura’s National Party: Juan Orlando Hernandez, who had been serving a 45-year sentence in the US after being convicted on drug trafficking and weapons charges.
Despite the charged environment, Tegucigalpa’s streets remained calm on Thursday as citizens awaited results.
The Honduran presidency is decided in a single round. The candidate with the most votes wins, even if the margin is narrow or the candidate falls short of a majority.
In previous election cycles, contested results have led to mass protests and violent crackdowns by security forces.

data.title: Hong Kong’s October retail sales figures underline need for change
data.description:
The latest monthly retail sales figures were up 6.9 per cent on a year ago but sent mixed messages to business and service providers. Provisional figures for the value of October’s sales were boosted by the 1.4 million mainland Chinese passenger trips to the city during the eight-day autumn break, coinciding with the “golden week” holiday. This was a 15 per cent increase on the number of visitors during the seven-day break a year earlier. But the value of retail spending did not keep pace, apparently reflecting changed shopping habits.
In other figures, the revised estimate for September showed retail sales rose by 6 per cent year on year. For the first 10 months of 2025, the value of total retail sales was comparable with a year earlier.
A government spokesperson said the retail recovery gathered strength in October, with sales of most broad retail outlet types continuing to grow. Improved local consumer sentiment and sustained growth in visitor arrivals should continue to support growth in retail businesses.
After netting out the effect of price changes, the provisional estimate of the volume of total retail sales in October increased by 5.3 per cent compared with a year earlier.
Of the total retail sales value in October, online transactions accounted for 14.6 per cent. The value of online retail sales in that month, about HK$5.2 billion, increased by 27.2 per cent year on year.
Retailers should heed the message implicit in the failure of increased spending in October to keep pace with the higher number of visitors, which implies that per-head spending fell. This underscores the change in spending behaviour among mainland visitors. The once familiar sight of big spenders with shopping bags from luxury stores has given way to a newer generation of tourists, on smaller budgets, who seem more interested in Instagrammable moments and experiences, and are happy eating in a dai pai dong and shopping in local stores like Hong Kong residents.
That puts pressure on retailers and service providers to adjust to the new generation of visitors and their spending habits. Changing travel and consumption behaviour makes adaptation and innovation a must rather than an option.

data.title: Admiral says there was no ‘kill them all’ order in boat attack but video alarms lawmakers
data.description:
A Navy admiral commanding the US military strikes on an alleged drug boat in the Caribbean told lawmakers on Thursday that there was no “kill them all” order from Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth but a stark video of the attack left grave questions as Congress scrutinises the campaign that killed two people.
Admiral Frank Bradley appeared for a series of closed-door classified briefings at the Capitol as lawmakers conduct an investigation after a report that he ordered the follow-on attack that killed two survivors to comply with Hegesth’s demands. Legal experts have said such a strike could be a violation of the laws of military warfare.
“Bradley was very clear that he was given no such order, to give no quarter or to kill them all,” said Senator Tom Cotton, who heads the Senate Intelligence Committee, as he exited a classified briefing.
While Arkansas congressman Cotton defended the attack, Democrats who were also briefed and saw video of the survivors being killed questioned the Trump administration’s rationale and said the incident was deeply concerning.
“The order was basically: Destroy the drugs, kill the 11 people on the boat,” said Washington congressman Adam Smith, the top Democrat on the House Armed Services Committee.
Smith, who is demanding further investigation, said the survivors were “basically two shirtless people clinging to the bow of a capsized and inoperable boat, drifting in the water – until the missiles come and kill them”.
The classified sessions with Bradley, alongside the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff General Dan Caine, provided fresh information at a crucial moment as Hegseth’s leadership comes under scrutiny. But they did little to resolve growing questions about the legal basis for US President Donald Trump’s extraordinary campaign to use war powers against suspected drug smugglers. So far, more than 80 people have been killed in some 20 strikes.
Lawmakers have not yet specifically authorised the use of military force against the alleged drug boats, and the Republican-controlled Congress has turned back attempts to put a check on Trump’s power to engage in the missile campaign, which Hegseth has vowed will continue. Several Democrats have called for Hegseth to resign.
What I saw in that room was one of the most troubling things I’ve seen in my time in public service

Lawmakers want a full accounting of the September 2 strike, which was the first in what has become a months-long series of US military attacks on vessels the US believes to be ferrying drugs in the Caribbean and Pacific waters. The Washington Post had reported that Bradley ordered the follow-on attack on the survivors.
But lawmakers who lead the House and Senate’s national security committees in Congress came away with different descriptions of what the two survivors were doing when they were killed.
Cotton said he saw them “trying to flip a boat loaded with drugs bound for United States back over so they could stay in the fight”.
He said there were “several minutes” between the first and second attacks, which consisted of four missile strikes. He said it was “gratifying” that the US military was taking “the battle” to cartels.
But Connecticut congressman Jim Himes, the top Democrat on the House Intelligence Committee, said: “What I saw in that room was one of the most troubling things I’ve seen in my time in public service.”
“You have two individuals in clear distress, without any means of locomotion, with a destroyed vessel,” he said, and who “were killed by the United States”.
The survivors did not issue any distress call or other communications, though lawmakers were told it appeared the people had a hand raised, “waving” at one point during the attacks, Smith said.
Smith acknowledged there was probably cocaine on the boat, but he objects to the Republican administration’s rationale for continued attacks on alleged drug runners who may or may not be heading to the United States.
“That’s really the core of the problem with all of this,” he said. “That incredibly broad definition, I think, is what sets in motion all of these problems about using lethal force and using the military.”

At the time of the attack, Bradley was the commander of Joint Special Operations Command, overseeing coordinated operations between the military’s elite special operations units at Fort Bragg in North Carolina. About a month after the strike, he was promoted to commander of US Special Operations Command.
His military career, spanning more than three decades, was mostly spent serving in the elite Navy Seals and commanding joint operations. He was among the first special forces officers to deploy to Afghanistan after the September 11 attacks. His latest promotion to admiral was approved by unanimous voice vote in the Senate this year, and Democratic and Republican senators praised his record.
Republican Senator Thom Tillis of North Carolina has described Bradley as among those who are “rock solid” and “the most extraordinary people that have ever served in the military”.
But lawmakers such as Tillis have also made it clear they expect a reckoning if it is found that survivors were targeted. “Anybody in the chain of command that was responsible for it, that had vision of it, needs to be held accountable,” he said.
Underpinning Trump’s campaign against suspected traffickers is his argument that drug cartels amount to armed combatants because their cargo poses a threat to American lives.
Democrats are demanding the release of the full video of the September 2 attack, as well as written records of the orders and any directives about the mission from Hegseth. None of the written orders or audio of verbal commands was shared with the lawmakers.
A White House Office of Legal Counsel memo providing a rationale for the strikes was dated after the fact, on September 5. That memo remains undisclosed, and Democrats want it released.
Obtaining further information, though, will largely depend on action from Republican lawmakers, who have majority control of the committees, a potentially painful prospect for them if it puts them at odds with the president.

data.title: Decoding Gen Alpha’s quirky slang, from ‘skibidi’ to ‘Italian brain rot’ and ‘6 7’
data.description:
“I am Hugo, and I’m pretty sure I’m the first ever person in my family to get my brain rotted,” says Hugo Lee, a nine-year-old student at Lion’s Club International School in Tin Shui Wai.
It might sound odd to boast about having a rotten brain, but Hugo and his classmate, 10-year-old Jesswin Kerung, proudly compete to list as many slang words as possible. They rattle off words that parents of toddlers and young teens may already know: “skibidi”, “rizz”, “negative aura”, and the hottest slang at the time of writing – since there seems to be something new every other day – “6 7”.
Older generations struggling to keep up with new lingo is nothing new. Back in 1992, the LA Times published “List Solves Mystery of Teen Slang”, pandering to parents and teachers of Gen X and young millennials baffled by their children’s evolving dialects.
Now, those very Gen Xers and millennials rely on articles like this one to decode their kids’ speech. Born between 2010 and 2024, the eldest Gen Alpha kids came into the world alongside iPads and Instagram. Growing up fully immersed in technology, especially during the Covid-19 pandemic when they were stuck at home glued to screens, it’s no surprise that these so-called “iPad kids” have developed a hyper-online way of speaking which older Gen Zers and millennials often jokingly refer to as “brain rot”.
Oxford University Press named “brain rot” its Word of the Year in 2024, defining it as “a perceived loss of intelligence or critical thinking skills due to consuming unchallenging or silly content”. Gen Z and Gen Alpha often use the term humorously and self-deprecatingly to describe their exposure to low-value online content and its effects.

The phenomenon of brain rot is believed to stem from the popular term “skibidi”, which is credited as the first slang exclusive to Gen Alpha. It goes back to an animated YouTube series called “Skibidi Toilet” created by Alexey Gerasimov (username DaFuq!?Boom!) in February 2023. The show features a surreal war between toilets with human heads and humanoid characters with device heads. These bizarre videos quickly went viral among Gen Alpha. To date, there are 79 episodes, some of which have racked up over 211 million views.
Uzon Thebe, a 12-year-old student at Wai Kiu College fluent in Gen Alpha slang, admits, “I don’t really know what ‘skibidi’ means – it can be good or bad. We just say it because it’s fun.”
Like “skibidi”, many Gen Alpha slang terms lack concrete meanings. For instance, “sigma”, originally used by the manosphere as an archetype of the “alpha male, lone wolf”, now means “cool” in Gen Alpha slang, while the phrase “what the sigma” reworks “what the heck”. The 2024 viral TikTok track “Sigma Boy” by Russian bloggers Betsy and Maria Iankovskaia further propelled the term’s popularity.
The current favourite, “6 7”, (pronounced six, seven) comes from the song Doot Doot (6 7) by Skrilla. Initially linked to basketball players’ heights, “6 7” became viral Gen Alpha slang after a video of a boy yelling the numbers while moving his hands up and down spread in March this year.

“If anyone mentions the number six or seven, my friends say ‘6 7’,” Uzon explains, mimicking the hand gesture. Even popular culture has taken note: South Park parodied the trend in an episode that aired in October.
Yashal Farooq, 23, a teacher at Lee’s school, says that despite being Gen Z herself, she struggles to stay current. “For us, a new word or trend pops up every few months. With Gen Alpha, there’s something new almost daily.”
Her colleague, Jamie Priestley, who has been teaching for over seven years, agrees that it gets harder to connect with students every year, though not for lack of trying. Both Priestley and Farooq say that they try to use slang when they’re talking to their students because they’ve noticed it helps them stay more engaged in class.
Nicholas Flood, 23, Thebe’s class teacher and English teacher, takes it a step further by weaving slang into lessons. “Once when we were practising using different tenses in class, I had the students conjugate using the verb ‘to mew’ and they thought this was really funny. I mean, it still teaches them what they need to learn, but it’s just using what they like and adapting it into the lesson,” says Flood. “The most important thing for me is that it makes them more interested in class.”

While teachers are willing to use slang occasionally in class, Farooq warns that students sometimes pick up inappropriate words from the internet, often without realising their impact. “I do try to discourage certain words like ‘big back’ which can be harmful because it encourages body shaming amongst children even if they are just joking around,” she says.
While both Priestley and Flood also discourage their students from using certain words in class, they believe there is a “time and place” to use slang.
“This might be a weird take,” prefaces Flood, who says he believes that children using inappropriate words is inevitable. “Even the parents reading this were probably using inappropriate words at that age. I feel like it’s less about stopping kids from saying it, but more about them understanding that there’s a time and a place.”
Among the various kinds of brain rot content online, the one that has caused most confusion for parents and teachers is “Italian brain rot”, a new video-meme format that was popularised on platforms like TikTok and Instagram reels in early 2025.
Even the parents reading this were probably using inappropriate words at that age
Nicholas Flood
The meme consists of AI-generated videos of hybrid animal characters with Italian sounding names, such as “Trallallero Trallallà”, which features a shark wearing trainers, and “Bombardino Cocodrilo”, a crocodile fused with a bomber plane. The result is a never-ending series of short, chaotic videos with odd visuals and AI-generated earworms, which according to Kerung is “super addicting”.
“They’ll come up to us and try to quiz us on Italian brain rot. Everyday there’s something new,” says Priestley.
Farooq, who has looked into Italian brain rot for the sake of understanding her students better, says she doesn’t see the appeal.
Dr Melissa Giglio, clinical psychologist and director of Central Health Partners’ Child Development Team, explains, “For neurotypical kids, these ‘Italian brain rot’ clips are a way to socially connect with their peers. Videos offer a sense of being in the know with what’s on trend, and the children learn new vocabulary to add to their everyday vernacular.”
But is “brain rot” just buzzword hype or is there real cause for concern? Giglio warns, “It’s called brain rot for a reason. These endless reels lack educational value. They can impact children’s attention span by flooding their brains with dopamine from rapid content-switching, which discourages cognitive activities like problem-solving, motor skills and deep focus.”

But beyond concerns about shortened attention spans and too much screen time, Giglio reminds us that every generation has always had its own collection of catchy sayings and phrases picked up from pop culture that are used in everyday conversations.
Although Gen Alpha’s slang and meme culture might sound like fast-changing gibberish to parents and teachers, that’s part of the point. Remember, these silly phrases are not so different from millennials who still love to quote “I volunteer as tribute,” from The Hunger Games or “Luke, I am your father,” from Star Wars.
Brain rot glossary
Skibidi
A meaningless adjective that can be paired with other slang
Rizz
Short for “charisma”
Negative aura
A bad vibe
Aura farming
Attention seeking
6, 7
A way of saying “meh” or “so-so”
Mew
The act of positioning your mouth to emphasise your jawline
Sigma
“Cool”
Chopped
To look bad or visually unappealing
Unc
An older person
Sus
Short for “suspicious”
No cap
To not be lying or exaggerating
Ohio
Something weird, awkward or cringe
Big back
Someone overweight or who eats a lot of food
Chicken jockey
A reference to a Minecraft monster wherein a baby zombie is riding a chicken

data.title: ‘Blue ocean market’: why Brazil is becoming a magnet for Chinese investment
data.description:
With their domestic profits narrowing and production capacity expanding, China’s firms are continuing to widen their overseas footprints in search of new, more lucrative markets. In this series, we examine China Inc.’s next phase of “going global” and the complex, challenging international environment its companies have chosen to enter.
In early November, the chairman of one of China’s top construction machinery makers was spotted touring potential factory sites in Piracicaba, a city in southeastern Brazil.
The visit was another in a string of recent moves by major Chinese companies in the South American nation: food delivery giant Meituan has entered the market via its overseas brand Keeta, electric car maker BYD has started operations at a huge new plant in the northeastern state of Bahia, and TikTok – owned by tech leader ByteDance – has pledged US$37.7 billion to build a data centre in Ceara, another northeastern state.
Perhaps previously best known in China for its national football team and majority share of the Amazon rainforest, Brazil is now emerging as a major investment hotspot, with Chinese companies of all sizes and types – state-owned energy giants, individual e-commerce vendors and everything in between – seeing its market as a fertile land of opportunity.
The Brazilian visa application centre in central Beijing is now packed most weekdays. Industry insiders say there is a real buzz about Brazil among Chinese investors at the moment.
“So many Chinese are coming to Brazil now,” said Jack Lee, founder of the US-based consulting firm Grand Central Advisory, which helps Chinese firms enter the South American market.
“It looks to me like they’re all here for business,” added Lee, who assisted the construction machinery company with site selection in Piracicaba, where the firm’s biggest client, Caterpillar, has a facility.
Chinese companies have shown greater interest in investing in Brazil ever since Beijing lifted its Covid-19 pandemic border controls in early 2023, but the trend has accelerated even further over the past 18 months, said Tian Bin, CEO at IEST Group, a consultancy focusing on Chinese businesses in Brazil.
In 2024, Chinese firms confirmed projects worth US$4.18 billion in Brazil, a 113 per cent increase from the year before, making the country the third-largest recipient of Chinese investment worldwide, according to a September report by the Brazil-China Business Council.
South America is probably the last untapped blue ocean market
Tian Bin, IEST Group
Tian said his company signed no more than 20 deals with Chinese companies looking to enter the Brazilian market in 2022. Last year, the number surpassed 50, and this year they had “already signed contracts with 60 to 70 companies while receiving over 200 inquiries” by November.
Unlike other Chinese investment hotspots, such as Mexico and Southeast Asia, most of these companies do not intend to use Brazil as a transit hub for goods destined for the United States. They are mostly attracted by Brazil itself – a destination they see as offering an enticing mix of advantages.
With a population of over 200 million, it is already a large market and is viewed as a gateway to the rest of South America. Many Chinese investors feel the region has vast untapped potential that they are well positioned to unlock, insiders say.
Brazil has a relatively weak manufacturing base along with underdeveloped supporting services and infrastructure – areas where Chinese companies excel. Moreover, few firms from China currently operate there, meaning early entrants will face less competition than in other markets.
“It is widely acknowledged [among Chinese investors] that South America is probably the last untapped blue ocean market,” Tian said. “Unlike Southeast Asia, which is close to China and has a large Chinese diaspora, leading to a significant Chinese presence there, Brazil currently hosts far fewer Chinese companies.”
At the same time, Chinese investors feel reassured by Brazil’s relatively cordial relations with Beijing and the presence of several Chinese industrial giants, the consultants said.
In Mexico, the energy supplier is usually the federal government. But in Brazil, it can be the State Grid of China
Jack Lee, Grand Central Advisory
State-owned energy firms like State Grid and China Petroleum and Chemical Corp (Sinopec) - as well as private telecoms giants Huawei Technologies and ZTE - began investing in Brazil a decade ago, and their success paved the way for smaller players, Lee said.
“For manufacturers doing site selection, real estate and energy supply are equally important. In Mexico, the energy supplier is usually the federal government. But in Brazil, it can be the State Grid of China.”
Meanwhile, Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva has prioritised strengthening economic ties with China since his return to office in 2023, calling the relationship “very strategic”. Brazil is China’s largest trading partner in South America and the two nations are founding members of the Brics bloc of emerging economies.
Another source of bilateral goodwill is the large trade surplus Brazil has with China, unlike the deficits of most other major economies, thanks to its large-scale exports of minerals and agricultural goods like soybeans.
“Some Chinese companies may be thinking, ‘Instead of us going to unfriendly or even adversarial countries, why don’t we go to more friendly countries like Brazil?’” Lee said.
Changzhou Xili Vehicle, a manufacturer of electric tricycles and other vehicles, is one of many companies asking that question. The Chinese firm is considering moving some production overseas to circumvent the higher import duties applied to fully assembled vehicles in several target markets, and Brazil is one of the destinations on its shortlist.
“The market of South America, especially Brazil, is among the largest,” said Amy Geng, the company’s foreign trade manager. “Brazil has a large population and is friendly towards China.”


For Geng, there is a five-year window of opportunity for electric vehicle makers in Brazil, as the country is actively embracing renewable energy. But smaller players like Xili do not have the financial resources of industry giants like BYD, meaning they often prefer to form joint ventures with local partners.
“Chinese producers would need to invest heavily and face significant uncertainties if they set up operations independently,” Geng said. “The safest approach is to partner with local distributors, with us as a shareholder providing technology and other support.”
“Building a factory will take one to two years, and the window is only five years. So, we need to be quick,” she added.
Chinese carmaker Geely has adopted a similar approach, announcing a partnership with French auto giant Renault Group in early November to manufacture and sell two models of new energy vehicles in Brazil.
The deal saw Geely take a 26.4 per cent stake in Renault’s Brazilian operations, with Geely providing access to its new energy technology in exchange for the use of Renault’s local production facility and distribution network.
The two carmakers have also begun shaping a strategy to attract more Chinese auto parts suppliers to the area surrounding their plant in the southern Brazilian state of Parana, said Renault brand CEO Fabrice Cambolive in an interview with Brazilian news outlet Valor Economico.
Many Chinese suppliers are already on the way, including tyre maker Linglong, which included plans for a US$1.19 billion plant in Parana in an April feasibility report. The project is undergoing various approval procedures, the company told investors in November.
Only companies with robust corporate governance capabilities can succeed in this market
Tian Bin, IEST Group
The wave of enthusiasm behind Chinese investment in Brazil could last at least two or three years, as more downstream suppliers move to the country to serve larger brands, according to IEST’s Tian.
Yet, the scale of Chinese investment in Brazil – especially in the industrial manufacturing sector – remains much smaller than in Mexico, despite the rapid growth seen over the past year, said Chen Gongen, president of the Chinese Chamber of Commerce of Brazil in Bahia.
“There’s no shortage of interest in building large-scale factories in Brazil, but few have progressed from plan to reality,” Chen said.
So far, many Chinese ventures in Brazil have focused on e-commerce, Chen said. That has led to a number of small Chinese-invested factories springing up in major cities, such as apparel printing facilities and plants for manufacturing plastic bags and packaging materials, he added.
Investing in Brazil also comes with risks, Chen said, including the possibility of sudden policy reversals. The Brazilian government has launched anti-dumping investigations into a series of Chinese goods in recent years, including some steel and fibre optic products.
Exchange rate volatility is another major risk for Chinese investors in the Brazilian market, Tian said.
“It is not a market where just any business can easily thrive, given Brazil’s complex business environment, which itself acts as a barrier to entry,” he said, adding that the country’s labyrinthine tax system and stronger labour protections could also trip up Chinese companies.
“Only companies with robust corporate governance capabilities can succeed in this market,” Tian said.
Additional reporting by He Huifeng

data.title: America’s opioid crisis won’t be solved without India’s help
data.description:
Much has been written about China’s alleged role in the US opioid crisis. Chemicals from Chinese suppliers have supposedly been used by Mexican cartels to produce fentanyl, which is then trafficked into the United States where it has fuelled a deadly overdose crisis.
Successive US administrations have pressured Beijing to crack down on its chemical industry. China has taken repeated steps to regulate precursors, including most recently last month when it restricted 13 new chemicals at US President Donald Trump’s request.
But less has been said about India’s role. Like China, it has a huge pharmaceutical and chemical sector that provides precursors for illicit drug manufacturers, while diverting pharmaceuticals, like tramadol, to the black market.
India’s role in the narcotics trade has prompted concern in Washington. In 2022, a bipartisan US opioid commission warned that India was “not far behind” China as a supplier of precursors.
“India also has large chemical and pharmaceutical sectors that are known to export synthetic drug precursors to trafficking organisations in Mexico. It could be only a matter of time before illegal production migrates to India or elsewhere,” the report noted.
According to officials cited in a 2022 Brookings Institution report, the illicit production of precursors in India and their export are “extensive” while “drug policy control in India is significantly below that of China”.


The US State Department noted this year that “India would benefit from updating its drug control regulation and closing loopholes that enable the illicit production and shipment of fentanyl analogues and other synthetic drugs”.
US intelligence annual threat assessments in 2024 and 2025 also mentioned India’s role as a chemical supplier for illicit drug manufacture. It was not mentioned in the 2023 report, pointing to fresh concerns in the American government.
And the US is starting to act. Three Indian chemical firms have been hit with American indictments this year. Trump also put India on a list of major illicit drug-producing or drug transit countries, and visas have been revoked for executives allegedly involved in trafficking precursors.
This pressure is important because illicit opioid producers can evade restrictions in China by sourcing their ingredients from India instead. This has happened before, when cartels looked for alternative suppliers after Beijing imposed new regulations, according to the US Drug Enforcement Administration.
And there is a lot more New Delhi can do to improve the situation. Its regulatory system is inadequate. For example, Beijing scheduled the entire class of fentanyl substances in 2019, something that India has not yet done.


India’s lead anti-drug agency is understaffed, and courts face a backlog of cases. Enforcement and monitoring are hampered by a chaotic system of overlapping central and local authorities. Indian bureaucracy is cumbersome and inefficient.
Precursor control is difficult at the best of times as most chemicals have legitimate uses and cannot be scheduled without harming the licit economy. And, if a substance is banned, illicit drug producers can devise novel chemical formulas using unscheduled ingredients.
That is why, despite its efforts, China has not managed to completely stem the flow of chemicals used in illicit fentanyl production in Mexico. Cartels have even hired chemists to synthesise drugs using unregulated, widely available precursors.
However, Beijing’s controls have disrupted fentanyl manufacture and made drug traffickers’ lives more difficult. But whatever benefits may have accrued from China’s regulations will go up in smoke if producers can simply shift their operations across the border to India.

It is vital that Delhi more aggressively cracks down on illicit precursor trafficking and tightens regulation of its chemical sector. Of course, reining in a profitable industry is not desirable for the Indian government.
But if it does not, the US may take matters into its own hands. There was already a close call earlier this year, when the Trump administration apparently considered using India’s role in fentanyl production as justification for tariffs.
Pressure from Washington appears to be paying off: Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi recently proposed a new anti-drug initiative at the G20 summit. India and America have cooperated well to address drugs in the past, and can do so again.
Ultimately, Delhi will have to do a lot more to assuage American concerns. And it’s not just the US: European countries are worried about the spread of opioids too, especially nitazenes, which are much stronger than fentanyl. China subjected nitazenes to class-wide controls this year; India has not yet done so.
Collaboration is essential to stop the world’s greatest ever drug overdose crisis from metastasising into something even worse.
